title: Storing and reconditioning clay
subtitle: Keeping unused clay workable, and rescuing clay that has gone too dry
excerpt: A working block of air-dry clay stays usable for weeks if it is stored correctly: airtight, with a damp insert to maintain moisture. Clay that has gone too dry can usually be brought back with a slow rehydration in a sealed bag. This tutorial walks through both the storage routine and the recovery method.
sourceNotes: Clay storage and reconditioning practice derived from first principles and traditional studio handling. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry, for the historical context of clay storage in damp-shed conditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of breaking a dry clay block down by soaking it in water until it crumbles back to a workable consistency.
Restoring stiffened or partly-dried clay to its original working consistency, usually by adding water and wedging.

aliases: how to store air dry clay

content.text: Air-dry clay loses moisture continuously, even inside its packaging once that has been opened. A few days of careless storage and a fresh block becomes unworkably stiff. Looked after properly, the same block stays usable for weeks. And clay that has already gone too dry can almost always be brought back. This is the storage routine and the recovery method.
Storing fresh clay between sessions

After working, the unused portion goes back into airtight storage. The original plastic bag works if it is still intact; cut it open along the seam carefully when first opened so the bag can be re-sealed. Add a second layer of protection (a sealed plastic box, a zip-top bag, or a sealed bucket with a lid) against the slow air leak that any single bag develops.

Inside the sealed container, place a damp insert: a wet sponge in a small dish, a damp cloth folded into the corner, or a small jar of water with the lid open. The insert keeps the air inside the container at high humidity, which slows moisture loss from the clay block. Check the insert each week and re-wet it.
Storing work in progress overnight

A piece that needs to stay workable for another session goes under a loose plastic bag, not a tight one. The loose bag holds the humidity around the piece without flattening any soft surfaces. A spritz of water from a spray bottle inside the bag adds extra moisture if the room is dry. For longer breaks of two or three days, wrap the piece in a damp cloth before bagging, refreshing the cloth each day.

Pieces stored under plastic for too long develop a soft surface scum and can mould over time. Two or three days is the usual practical limit; beyond that, either let the piece dry to bone-dry and finish another session, or break it down for clay reclaim.
Spotting clay that needs reconditioning

Workable air-dry clay feels firm but yielding under thumb pressure, with a uniform surface texture and no dry crust. The thumb leaves a clear print. Clay that has stiffened slightly still presses but resists more than usual; clay that has gone too dry resists hard, cracks under the thumb, and shows a pale dry outer skin.

Slice through a suspect block with the wire cutter and look at the cross-section. A workable block is uniform in colour throughout. A stiffened block shows a darker damp core and a paler dry outer ring. The depth of the dry ring tells you how much rehydration is needed.
Reconditioning slightly stiff clay
For clay that is firm but not yet dry-cracked,
reconditioning

takes a few hours. Cut the block into 30 mm cubes with the wire cutter. Spray each cube with water on every face, a fine mist rather than a soak. Reassemble the cubes loosely in a sealed plastic bag with a damp cloth tucked in beside them. Leave for 6-12 hours. The water migrates inward through the cubes; the bag and cloth keep the surface from drying again. Wedge the reassembled clay for three or four minutes to even out the moisture, and the block is usable again.
Rescuing clay that has gone fully dry
Clay that has dried out completely needs slaking.
Slaking

is breaking the dry clay back down by submerging it in water until it crumbles. Break the dry block into walnut-sized chunks with a hammer if needed. Place the chunks in a plastic tub and cover with water by 20 mm above the highest piece. Leave for 24-48 hours, undisturbed. The dry clay absorbs water and falls apart into a soupy mass.

Pour off any surface water and tip the slurry onto a plaster bat or a thick stack of newspaper covered with cloth. The plaster (or the absorbent paper) draws the excess water out from underneath. Check the consistency after 4-8 hours; the clay is ready when it is firm enough to lift in a single mass without sticking to the surface. Wedge thoroughly for five minutes to redistribute moisture before using.
Paper clay and polymer clay
Paper clay handles like air-dry clay for storage and reconditioning, with the bonus that the paper fibres help reabsorb water more evenly during slaking. The same routine applies.

Polymer clay does not dry and does not need rehydration. Stored at room temperature in its original packaging, polymer clay stays workable for years. The only storage concern is keeping it cool: prolonged storage above 25°C can soften the body and start the curing process slowly. Avoid storing polymer clay in direct sun, near a radiator, or in a hot car.
Common mistakes

Soaking a stiff clay block whole in water. The outside reaches saturation while the inside stays dry, and the rehydrated layer becomes too soft to handle while the core is still too hard. Slaking works only on fully dry clay broken into small pieces; on partly dry clay, the misting-and-bagging method is better.
Trying to wedge water into hard clay. The water sits on the outer surface and refuses to penetrate. Cut, mist, bag, and wait first; only wedge after the moisture has had time to migrate inward.
Storing clay in a paper bag inside a plastic box. The paper bag wicks moisture away from the clay and accelerates drying. Use plastic-on-plastic only.

items.symptom: Stored clay smells unpleasant after a few weeks
items.cause: Mould has developed in the high-humidity environment
items.fix: Mould does not harm the working properties; wedge the clay thoroughly to break up any visible mould and use as normal

items.symptom: Slaked clay stays too wet after 12 hours on plaster
items.cause: Plaster saturated, or slurry layer too thick
items.fix: Move to a fresh dry surface or spread thinner; check progress hourly once it nears workable stiffness

items.cause: Moisture not evenly redistributed after recovery
items.fix: Wedge for a full five minutes before use; cut and inspect the cross-section to confirm uniform colour and texture
